title: Greek Lemon Chicken Soup

ingredients.name: chicken stock (or stock cube in 1.2 litres hot water)

subs.name: Homemade chicken broth is ideal; Maggi or Knorr chicken stock cubes dissolved in 1.2 litres of hot water give a very acceptable result
Fine vermicelli (sevai) broken into small pieces gives a thinner but equally comforting soup; orzo pasta (if available) is traditional in some Greek versions
The eggs are fundamental to avgolemono — without them, the dish becomes a simple lemon chicken soup. One additional yolk gives extra richness and stability
The lemon quantity can be adjusted to taste; a very tart lemon may need only 1.5 pieces while a mild one may need 2.5 for the right brightness

steps.text: Pour the stock into a large pot and bring to a boil. Add the whole chicken pieces and the rice, season with salt, and reduce the heat to a simmer. Cook for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the rice is completely soft.
Lift the chicken out with tongs and shred it into fine strips using two forks. Return the shredded chicken to the pot and keep the soup at a very gentle simmer — it should barely be moving.

In a bowl, beat the eggs and lemon juice together vigorously with a fork or whisk until the mixture is pale, slightly frothy, and fully combined. This is the avgolemono — the egg-lemon emulsion that will thicken and brighten the soup.

Slowly ladle about 2–3 cups of the hot soup liquid into the egg-lemon bowl, a small amount at a time, whisking constantly as you pour. This is called tempering — it gradually raises the temperature of the eggs to prevent them from scrambling when they enter the hot soup.

Pour the tempered egg-lemon mixture back into the pot of soup in a slow stream while stirring continuously. Keep the heat on the absolute lowest setting and stir gently for 3–5 minutes — the soup will thicken slightly and turn a gorgeous silky pale-gold. Do not let it boil at this point or the eggs will curdle.

Taste for salt, add a good grind of black pepper, and serve immediately in warm bowls with fresh parsley scattered on top and an extra wedge of lemon on the side. The soup is best eaten right away as it can lose its silky texture if reheated to a full boil.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: stock → Homemade chicken broth is ideal; Maggi or Knorr chicken stock cubes dissolved in 1.2 litres of hot water give a very acceptable result; rice → Fine vermicelli (sevai) broken into small pieces gives a thinner but equally comforting soup; orzo pasta (if available) is traditional in some Greek versions; egg → The eggs are fundamental to avgolemono — without them, the dish becomes a simple lemon chicken soup. One additional yolk gives extra richness and stability; lemon → The lemon quantity can be adjusted to taste; a very tart lemon may need only 1.5 pieces while a mild one may need 2.5 for the right brightness
indian_kitchen_adaptation: The key Indian technique to know here is tempering eggs with hot liquid — it is the same principle as adding hot oil to a beaten egg in French cooking but done the opposite way around. Keep the flame very low after adding the avgolemono mixture; Indian gas burners can run very hot so use the smallest burner at the lowest setting. Chicken stock cubes (Maggi, Knorr, or Real Good) are widely available and make this achievable in 45 minutes from a cold start. Semiya (vermicelli) is a very common Indian substitute for the rice and works beautifully in this soup.
